title: Imagining democratic insurgency: enthusiasm, sublimity, and finitude
author: White

opening_move.description: The paper diagnoses a gap in accounts of sublimity in political theory: recent aesthetico-affective approaches treat sublimity as a dissensual, disruptive force, but neglect an older tradition linking sublimity to democratic collective binding — and neither tradition can adequately distinguish democratic from fascist enthusiasm.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Without a 'bivalent sublimity' encompassing both enthusiasm and sobering finitude, democratic insurgency cannot be distinguished from proto-fascist collective energism

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper engages Connolly's political aesthetics in a dual mode — critical of the undertheorised distinction between democratic and fascist enthusiasm, but constructive in drawing on Connolly's identity/difference ontology to build a 'sobering sublimity' out of finitude.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Kant, Connolly, Burke, Sorel, Nietzsche; political-aesthetic analysis; 18th-century intellectual history
epistemic_mode.notes: Uses historical/philosophical cases (Robespierre, fascist aesthetics, Msezane's performance art, MLK's letter from Birmingham) as illustrative interpretive material, not systematic evidence.

case_type.case_name: Connolly's myth of the general strike; fascist rally aesthetics; Msezane's performance art; MLK's Letter from Birmingham Jail

confidence_notes: Full-length research article (19 pages). The argument is fully normative-interpretive: no empirical data but extensive philosophical case analysis. The 'interpretive case' designation reflects use of historical examples as theoretical lenses rather than constitutive evidence.
